1.title: 1st person
1.html: Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

2.title: 2nd person
2.html: Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3.title: 3rd person
3.html: Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There's no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

hausdorff.title: Hausdorff
hausdorff.html: A topological space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points p,qp, qp,q have disjoint open neighborhoods U∋pU \ni pU∋p, V∋qV \ni qV∋q with U∩V=∅U \cap V = \emptysetU∩V=∅. Most spaces appearing in differential geometry are Hausdorff: Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, every metric space, every manifold, every Lie group. The non-Hausdorff cases are exotic — the “line with two origins” is the canonical example.

second-countable.title: Second countable
second-countable.html: A topological space is second countable if its topology has a countable base: countably many open sets such that every open set is a union of some of them. Equivalently for metric spaces, XXX has a countable dense subset and balls of rational radius centered on that subset form a base. This is what makes paracompactness and partition-of-unity arguments work on a manifold.

embedded-manifold.title: Embedded manifold
embedded-manifold.html:
A smooth submanifold of RN\mathbb{R}^NRN is a subset M⊆RNM \subseteq \mathbb{R}^NM⊆RN that locally looks like the graph of a smooth function: around every p∈Mp \in Mp∈M there is an open U⊆RNU \subseteq \mathbb{R}^NU⊆RN, an open V⊆RnV \subseteq \mathbb{R}^nV⊆Rn, and a smooth embedding Φ:V→U∩M\Phi: V \to U \cap MΦ:V→U∩M whose differential is everywhere injective. The image Φ(V)\Phi(V)Φ(V) is an open piece of MMM, and the nnn coordinates on VVV are a chart on MMM.

The embedded view of differential geometry takes advantage of the ambient RN\mathbb{R}^NRN: tangent vectors are vectors in RN\mathbb{R}^NRN (sitting in the affine tangent plane to MMM); the metric is the pullback of the Euclidean inner product; integration is over an embedded submanifold of RN\mathbb{R}^NRN. The Whitney embedding theorem says every smooth nnn-manifold can be embedded in R2n\mathbb{R}^{2n}R2n, so the embedded view loses no generality in principle. In practice it can be inconvenient — Lorentzian spacetimes do not embed isometrically in any flat ambient — which is why the abstract view is also needed.

abstract-manifold.title: Abstract manifold
abstract-manifold.html:
An abstract smooth manifold is a , topological space equipped with an atlas of charts φα:Uα→Rn\varphi_\alpha: U_\alpha \to \mathbb{R}^nφα​:Uα​→Rn whose transition maps φβ∘φα−1\varphi_\beta \circ \varphi_\alpha^{-1}φβ​∘φα−1​ are smooth. No ambient space; the manifold is the chart-and-transition data, considered up to refinement of atlas.

All structures — tangent spaces, tensor fields, metrics, connections — are then built intrinsically. The geometry in this book is mostly abstract, with the embedded view brought in as the geometrically transparent special case.

derivation.title: Derivation
derivation.html:
For a commutative algebra AAA over R\mathbb{R}R (e.g., A=C∞(M)A = C^\infty(M)A=C∞(M)), a derivation at p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is an R\mathbb{R}R-linear map D:A→RD: A \to \mathbb{R}D:A→R satisfying the Leibniz rule

D(fg)=f(p) D(g)+g(p) D(f).D(fg) = f(p)\, D(g) + g(p)\, D(f). D(fg)=f(p)D(g)+g(p)D(f).

Every derivation at ppp is determined by its values on a coordinate system around ppp — D(xμ)D(x^\mu)D(xμ) for the chart x=(x1,…,xn)x = (x^1, \ldots, x^n)x=(x1,…,xn) — and the space of derivations is an nnn-dimensional real vector space, isomorphic to TpMT_p MTp​M.

The derivation viewpoint generalizes: a derivation of AAA (with values in AAA) is a vector field; the Lie bracket of vector fields is the commutator of derivations.

dual-space.title: Dual space
dual-space.html:
For a vector space VVV over a field kkk, the dual space is the space of kkk-linear functionals

V∗:=Homk(V,k).V^* := \mathrm{Hom}_k(V, k). V∗:=Homk​(V,k).

For finite-dimensional VVV, dim⁡V∗=dim⁡V\dim V^* = \dim VdimV∗=dimV; a basis {ei}\{e_i\}{ei​} of VVV induces a dual basis {ei}\{e^i\}{ei} of V∗V^*V∗ defined by ei(ej)=δjie^i(e_j) = \delta^i_jei(ej​)=δji​. The double dual is canonically isomorphic to VVV: V∗∗≅VV^{**} \cong VV∗∗≅V via v↦(ω↦ω(v))v \mapsto (\omega \mapsto \omega(v))v↦(ω↦ω(v)). The cotangent space Tp∗MT^*_p MTp∗​M is the dual of the tangent space TpMT_p MTp​M.

lie-algebra.title: Lie algebra
lie-algebra.html:
A Lie algebra over R\mathbb{R}R is a real vector space g\mathfrak{g}g equipped with a bilinear antisymmetric bracket [⋅,⋅]:g×g→g[\cdot, \cdot]: \mathfrak{g} \times \mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}[⋅,⋅]:g×g→g satisfying the Jacobi identity

[X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0.[X, [Y, Z]] + [Y, [Z, X]] + [Z, [X, Y]] = 0. [X,[Y,Z]]+[Y,[Z,X]]+[Z,[X,Y]]=0.

Examples: gln(R)\mathfrak{gl}_n(\mathbb{R})gln​(R) — n×nn \times nn×n real matrices with [A,B]=AB−BA[A, B] = AB - BA[A,B]=AB−BA; the tangent space at the identity of any Lie group; the vector fields on a manifold under the Lie bracket. The vector-fields Lie algebra X(M)\mathfrak{X}(M)X(M) is infinite-dimensional and not the Lie algebra of any finite-dimensional Lie group.

tensor-field.title: Tensor field
tensor-field.html:
A tensor field of type (r,s)(r, s)(r,s) on a smooth manifold MMM is a smooth section of the bundle whose fiber at p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is

TpM⊗r⊗Tp∗M⊗s.T_p M^{\otimes r} \otimes T^*_p M^{\otimes s}. Tp​M⊗r⊗Tp∗​M⊗s.

Equivalently, a C∞(M)C^\infty(M)C∞(M)-multilinear map taking rrr smooth 111-forms and sss smooth vector fields and returning a smooth real-valued function. Vector fields are (1,0)(1, 0)(1,0)-tensor fields; 111-forms are (0,1)(0, 1)(0,1)-tensor fields; kkk-forms are alternating (0,k)(0, k)(0,k)-tensor fields; the metric is a symmetric (0,2)(0, 2)(0,2)-tensor field. The Lie derivative and (given a connection) the covariant derivative extend to tensor fields of every type.

einstein-summation.title: Einstein summation
einstein-summation.html:
The convention, due to Einstein and unstated everywhere it’s in force: a Greek (or Latin) index appearing exactly once up and exactly once down in the same monomial is summed over its range. So

vμωμ:=∑μvμωμ,Tμν Sνρ:=∑νTμν Sνρ.v^\mu \omega_\mu := \sum_{\mu} v^\mu \omega_\mu, \qquad T^\mu{}_ u\, S^ u{}_ ho := \sum_{ u} T^\mu{}_ u\, S^ u{}_ ho. vμωμ​:=μ∑​vμωμ​,Tμν​Sνρ​:=ν∑​Tμν​Sνρ​.

Two ramifications. First, repeated indices in the same vertical position (vμωμv^\mu \omega^\muvμωμ, TμμT_{\mu\mu}Tμμ​) are almost always an error — there is no canonical pairing without a metric. Second, an index used as a dummy variable is bound and can be renamed: vμωμ=vνωνv^\mu \omega_\mu = v^ u \omega_ uvμωμ​=vνων​. Be wary of dummy collisions when chaining contractions.

running-example.title: Running example
running-example.html:
The unit sphere

S2={(X,Y,Z)∈R3:X2+Y2+Z2=1}S^2 = \{ (X, Y, Z) \in \mathbb{R}^3 : X^2 + Y^2 + Z^2 = 1 \} S2={(X,Y,Z)∈R3:X2+Y2+Z2=1}

is the running example carried through Part II of this book. Two reasons it works:

  • Small enough to compute on by hand. Two coordinates, one round metric, four-line Christoffel calculation, single Riemann component.
  • Big enough to break intuition. Non-zero constant curvature; no global flat chart; non-trivial topology (Euler characteristic 222); SO(3)SO(3)SO(3) symmetry; closed orientable.

The drawback — S2S^2S2 is two-dimensional Riemannian, not four-dimensional Lorentzian — means it doesn’t model spacetime. But every tensor construction in the book is dimension- and signature-agnostic; correctness on S2S^2S2 is correctness in general.

wedge-convention.title: Wedge convention
wedge-convention.html:
This book writes a kkk-form in the 1/k!1/k!1/k! convention:

ω=1k! ωμ1⋯μk dxμ1∧⋯∧dxμk,\omega = \frac{1}{k!}\, \omega_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_k}\, dx^{\mu_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{\mu_k}, ω=k!1​ωμ1​⋯μk​​dxμ1​∧⋯∧dxμk​,

with ωμ1⋯μk\omega_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_k}ωμ1​⋯μk​​ totally antisymmetric and the sum over all index tuples. The common alternative — the strictly-increasing-index convention —

ω=∑μ1<⋯<μkωμ1⋯μk dxμ1∧⋯∧dxμk\omega = \sum_{\mu_1 < \cdots < \mu_k} \omega_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_k}\, dx^{\mu_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{\mu_k} ω=μ1​<⋯<μk​∑​ωμ1​⋯μk​​dxμ1​∧⋯∧dxμk​

carries no combinatorial prefactor and sums only over ordered tuples. The two give the same component array on ordered tuples (e.g. ωθφ=sin⁡θ\omega_{\theta\varphi} = \sin\thetaωθφ​=sinθ for the sphere’s area form either way); they differ only in whether the basis is reduced to ordered tuples or the components are antisymmetrized and divided by k!k!k!. Watch for the factor when comparing formulas across textbooks.

levi-civita.title: Levi-Civita
levi-civita.html:
Three related but distinct objects share the name.

Levi-Civita symbol. The totally antisymmetric symbol εμ1⋯μn\varepsilon_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_n}εμ1​⋯μn​​ — not a tensor — defined by ε1⋯n=1\varepsilon_{1 \cdots n} = 1ε1⋯n​=1 and antisymmetry under any pair swap. Coordinate-dependent: it transforms like a tensor density of weight +1+1+1. Used in the explicit formula for the Hodge star.

Levi-Civita tensor. The honest tensor ϵμ1⋯μn:=∣det⁡g∣ εμ1⋯μn\epsilon_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_n} := \sqrt{|\det g|}\, \varepsilon_{\mu_1 \cdots \mu_n}ϵμ1​⋯μn​​:=∣detg∣​εμ1​⋯μn​​. The factor of ∣det⁡g∣\sqrt{|\det g|}∣detg∣​ converts the density into a tensor; this is the canonical volume form volg\mathrm{vol}_gvolg​.

Levi-Civita connection. The unique torsion-free metric-compatible connection of a pseudo-Riemannian manifold (M,g)(M, g)(M,g). The connection assumed throughout standard GR. Christoffel symbols given by the formula in the covariant-derivative page.

Three different objects, one Italian mathematician. Context picks which is meant.

killing-vector.title: Killing vector
killing-vector.html:
A vector field K∈X(M)K \in \mathfrak{X}(M)K∈X(M) on a pseudo-Riemannian manifold (M,g)(M, g)(M,g) is a Killing vector field if its flow preserves the metric, i.e. if the Lie derivative

LKg=0.\mathcal{L}_K g = 0. LK​g=0.

In components,

∇μKν+∇νKμ=0\nabla_\mu K_\nu + \nabla_\nu K_\mu = 0 ∇μ​Kν​+∇ν​Kμ​=0

(the Killing equation, equivalent to LKg=0\mathcal{L}_K g = 0LK​g=0 for the Levi-Civita connection).

Killing fields are the infinitesimal generators of isometries: their flows are one-parameter families of isometries of (M,g)(M, g)(M,g). On S2S^2S2 with the round metric, the three generators of SO(3)SO(3)SO(3) give three Killing fields; in spherical coordinates, ∂φ\partial_\varphi∂φ​ is one of them (rotation about the ZZZ-axis).

Along a geodesic γ\gammaγ, Kμγ˙μK_\mu \dot\gamma^\muKμ​γ˙​μ is conserved — every Killing vector gives a conserved quantity for free-fall motion. This is the geodesic instance of Noether’s theorem: a Killing vector is a continuous symmetry of the geodesic action ∫gμνx˙μx˙ν dλ\int g_{\mu\nu}\dot x^\mu \dot x^\nu\, d\lambda∫gμν​x˙μx˙νdλ, and Kμγ˙μK_\mu \dot\gamma^\muKμ​γ˙​μ is the Noether charge (the classical-mechanics review has the general theorem). In Schwarzschild, ∂t\partial_t∂t​ and ∂φ\partial_\varphi∂φ​ are Killing; the two conserved quantities they yield are the energy EEE and angular momentum LLL that drive the orbit calculation.

cochain-complex.title: Cochain complex
cochain-complex.html:
A cochain complex is a sequence of abelian groups (or vector spaces) and linear maps

⋯→Ck−1→dk−1Ck→dkCk+1→⋯\cdots \to C^{k-1} \xrightarrow{d^{k-1}} C^k \xrightarrow{d^k} C^{k+1} \to \cdots ⋯→Ck−1dk−1​Ckdk​Ck+1→⋯

with dk∘dk−1=0d^k \circ d^{k-1} = 0dk∘dk−1=0 for every kkk. The kernel of each dkd^kdk (“cocycles”) contains the image of the previous dk−1d^{k-1}dk−1 (“coboundaries”), and the quotient ker⁡(dk)/im(dk−1)\ker(d^k) / \mathrm{im}(d^{k-1})ker(dk)/im(dk−1) is the kkk-th cohomology of the complex. The complex Ω0→Ω1→Ω2→⋯\Omega^0 \to \Omega^1 \to \Omega^2 \to \cdotsΩ0→Ω1→Ω2→⋯ with exterior derivative as ddd is the de Rham complex.

singular-cohomology.title: Singular cohomology
singular-cohomology.html: Singular cohomology Hk(X;A)H^k(X; A)Hk(X;A) of a topological space XXX with coefficients in an abelian group AAA is built from the dual of the singular . A singular kkk-simplex in XXX is a continuous map Δk→X\Delta^k \to XΔk→X; the free abelian group on these forms Ck(X)C_k(X)Ck​(X), with the boundary map ∂k:Ck→Ck−1\partial_k: C_k \to C_{k-1}∂k​:Ck​→Ck−1​. Then Ck(X;A):=Hom(Ck(X),A)C^k(X; A) := \mathrm{Hom}(C_k(X), A)Ck(X;A):=Hom(Ck​(X),A) and d:=∂∗d := \partial^*d:=∂∗. Cohomology depends only on the homotopy type of XXX, and for paracompact XXX the singular and de Rham versions agree (de Rham’s theorem).

star-shaped.title: Star-shaped
star-shaped.html: An open set U⊆RnU \subseteq \mathbb{R}^nU⊆Rn is star-shaped about p∈Up \in Up∈U if for every q∈Uq \in Uq∈U, the line segment {(1−t)p+tq:t∈[0,1]}\{(1-t)p + tq : t \in [0, 1]\}{(1−t)p+tq:t∈[0,1]} from ppp to qqq lies entirely in UUU. Convex sets are star-shaped about any of their points. Star-shaped sets are contractible — the homotopy H(q,t)=(1−t)q+tpH(q, t) = (1-t)q + tpH(q,t)=(1−t)q+tp retracts UUU to ppp — and this contraction is the ingredient that makes the cone-operator proof of the Poincaré lemma work.

sheaf.title: Sheaf
sheaf.html:
A sheaf of abelian groups on a topological space XXX assigns to every open U⊆XU \subseteq XU⊆X an abelian group F(U)\mathcal{F}(U)F(U) (“sections over UUU”) and to every inclusion V⊆UV \subseteq UV⊆U a restriction map F(U)→F(V)\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(V)F(U)→F(V), subject to a gluing axiom: local sections that agree on overlaps glue uniquely to a section on the union.

The constant sheaf A‾\underline{A}A​ assigns AAA (with the discrete topology) to each connected open set. The sheaf CM∞C^\infty_MCM∞​ assigns C∞(U)C^\infty(U)C∞(U) to each UUU. Sheaf cohomology Hk(X;F)H^k(X; \mathcal{F})Hk(X;F) generalizes singular cohomology; an acyclic resolution is a long exact sequence of sheaves that’s nice enough to compute cohomology from. The de Rham complex is an acyclic resolution of R‾\underline{\mathbb{R}}R​ on MMM, which is the abstract reason de Rham cohomology computes H∗(M;R)H^*(M; \mathbb{R})H∗(M;R).

noun.title: Noun
noun.html:
A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. In Latin, nouns inflect for (six forms — nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, ablative, vocative) and (singular and plural). Every noun belongs to one of five and carries a fixed (masculine, feminine, or neuter), shown in the dictionary entry.

Allen & Greenough: §36

verb.title: Verb
verb.html:
A word that names an action or a state. A Latin verb inflects for , , , , and . One verb has roughly 150 distinct forms; the four listed in the dictionary entry are enough to generate them all.

Allen & Greenough: §153

adj.title: Adjective
adj.html:
A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

Allen & Greenough: §109

adjective.title: Adjective
adjective.html:
A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

Allen & Greenough: §109

pron.title: Pronoun
pron.html:
A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

Allen & Greenough: §140

pronoun.title: Pronoun
pronoun.html:
A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

Allen & Greenough: §140

prep.title: Preposition
prep.html:
An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

Allen & Greenough: §219

preposition.title: Preposition
preposition.html:
An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

Allen & Greenough: §219

conj.title: Conjunction
conj.html:
An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

Allen & Greenough: §222

conjunction.title: Conjunction
conjunction.html:
An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

Allen & Greenough: §222

adv.title: Adverb
adv.html:
An indeclinable word that modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb (celeriter, “quickly”; iam, “already”). Latin adverbs do not inflect, though many form comparative and superlative degrees.

Allen & Greenough: §213

adverb.title: Adverb
adverb.html:
An indeclinable word that modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb (celeriter, “quickly”; iam, “already”). Latin adverbs do not inflect, though many form comparative and superlative degrees.

Allen & Greenough: §213

interj.title: Interjection
interj.html:
An invariant exclamatory word, used to express emotion or call attention (o!, “oh!”; ecce, “behold!”). Interjections stand outside the grammatical structure of the sentence.

Allen & Greenough: §225

interjection.title: Interjection
interjection.html:
An invariant exclamatory word, used to express emotion or call attention (o!, “oh!”; ecce, “behold!”). Interjections stand outside the grammatical structure of the sentence.

Allen & Greenough: §225

enclitic.title: Enclitic
enclitic.html: A particle attached to the end of the preceding word rather than written separately (-que = “and”; -ne = question marker; -ve = “or”). Primaque in dictionary order is prima + -que.

num.title: Numeral
num.html:
A word that denotes number — cardinal (unus, duo, tres; “one”, “two”, “three”), ordinal (primus, secundus; “first”, “second”), distributive (singuli, bini; “one each”, “two each”), or multiplicative (semel, bis; “once”, “twice”). Some are indeclinable; unus, duo, tres and the hundreds inflect.

Allen & Greenough: §132

numeral.title: Numeral
numeral.html:
A word that denotes number — cardinal (unus, duo, tres; “one”, “two”, “three”), ordinal (primus, secundus; “first”, “second”), distributive (singuli, bini; “one each”, “two each”), or multiplicative (semel, bis; “once”, “twice”). Some are indeclinable; unus, duo, tres and the hundreds inflect.

Allen & Greenough: §132

nom.title: Nominative
nom.html:
The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It's also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

Allen & Greenough: §339

nominative.title: Nominative
nominative.html:
The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It's also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

Allen & Greenough: §339

gen.title: Genitive
gen.html:
The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl's book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun's dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

Allen & Greenough: §341

genitive.title: Genitive
genitive.html:
The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl's book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun's dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

Allen & Greenough: §341

dat.title: Dative
dat.html:
The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

Allen & Greenough: §360

dative.title: Dative
dative.html:
The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

Allen & Greenough: §360

acc.title: Accusative
acc.html:
The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

Allen & Greenough: §386

accusative.title: Accusative
accusative.html:
The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

Allen & Greenough: §386

abl.title: Ablative
abl.html:
A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

Allen & Greenough: §398

ablative.title: Ablative
ablative.html:
A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

Allen & Greenough: §398

voc.title: Vocative
voc.html:
The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

Allen & Greenough: §340

vocative.title: Vocative
vocative.html:
The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

Allen & Greenough: §340

sg.title: Singular
sg.html:
A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

Allen & Greenough: §35

singular.title: Singular
singular.html:
A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

Allen & Greenough: §35

pl.title: Plural
pl.html:
A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

Allen & Greenough: §35

plural.title: Plural
plural.html:
A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

Allen & Greenough: §35

masc.title: Masculine
masc.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that's just a fact about them; it doesn't always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

Allen & Greenough: §29

masculine.title: Masculine
masculine.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that's just a fact about them; it doesn't always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

Allen & Greenough: §29

fem.title: Feminine
fem.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

Allen & Greenough: §29

feminine.title: Feminine
feminine.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

Allen & Greenough: §29

neut.title: Neuter
neut.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

Allen & Greenough: §29

neuter.title: Neuter
neuter.html:
One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

Allen & Greenough: §29

1st-person.title: 1st person
1st-person.html: Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

2nd-person.title: 2nd person
2nd-person.html: Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3rd-person.title: 3rd person
3rd-person.html: Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There's no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

pres.title: Present
pres.html:
The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

Allen & Greenough: §464

present.title: Present
present.html:
The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

Allen & Greenough: §464

imperf.title: Imperfect
imperf.html:
The past tense of ongoing or repeated action (amabat, “he was loving” / “he used to love”). Built on the present stem with -ba- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

imperfect.title: Imperfect
imperfect.html:
The past tense of ongoing or repeated action (amabat, “he was loving” / “he used to love”). Built on the present stem with -ba- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

fut.title: Future
fut.html:
The tense of action yet to happen (amabit, “he will love”). Different conjugations form the future with different endings.

Allen & Greenough: §464

future.title: Future
future.html:
The tense of action yet to happen (amabit, “he will love”). Different conjugations form the future with different endings.

Allen & Greenough: §464

perf.title: Perfect
perf.html:
The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

Allen & Greenough: §464

perfect.title: Perfect
perfect.html:
The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

Allen & Greenough: §464

plup.title: Pluperfect
plup.html:
The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

pluperfect.title: Pluperfect
pluperfect.html:
The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

futperf.title: Future perfect
futperf.html:
The tense of action that will have been completed by some future point (amaverit, “he will have loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -eri- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

future-perfect.title: Future perfect
future-perfect.html:
The tense of action that will have been completed by some future point (amaverit, “he will have loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -eri- infix.

Allen & Greenough: §464

ind.title: Indicative
ind.html:
The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

Allen & Greenough: §437

indicative.title: Indicative
indicative.html:
The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

Allen & Greenough: §437

subj.title: Subjunctive
subj.html:
The mood of possibility, wish, command in subordinate clauses, hypothetical condition, and other non-assertive uses (amet, “may he love” / “let him love”). One of the most flexible features of Latin.

Allen & Greenough: §438

subjunctive.title: Subjunctive
subjunctive.html:
The mood of possibility, wish, command in subordinate clauses, hypothetical condition, and other non-assertive uses (amet, “may he love” / “let him love”). One of the most flexible features of Latin.

Allen & Greenough: §438

imp.title: Imperative
imp.html:
The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

Allen & Greenough: §448

imperative.title: Imperative
imperative.html:
The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

Allen & Greenough: §448

inf.title: Infinitive
inf.html:
A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb's dictionary entry.

Allen & Greenough: §451

infinitive.title: Infinitive
infinitive.html:
A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb's dictionary entry.

Allen & Greenough: §451

pap.title: Present active participle
pap.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing ongoing action by the subject (amans, “loving”). It declines as a third-declension adjective with one ending, agreeing with its noun in case, number, and gender, and is built on the present stem.

Allen & Greenough: §488

present-active-participle.title: Present active participle
present-active-participle.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing ongoing action by the subject (amans, “loving”). It declines as a third-declension adjective with one ending, agreeing with its noun in case, number, and gender, and is built on the present stem.

Allen & Greenough: §488

ppp.title: Perfect passive participle
ppp.html:
The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb's passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

Allen & Greenough: §488

perfect-passive-participle.title: Perfect passive participle
perfect-passive-participle.html:
The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb's passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

Allen & Greenough: §488

fap.title: Future active participle
fap.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing intended or impending action by the subject (amaturus, “about to love”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and combines with forms of esse to make the active periphrastic conjugation.

Allen & Greenough: §488

future-active-participle.title: Future active participle
future-active-participle.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing intended or impending action by the subject (amaturus, “about to love”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and combines with forms of esse to make the active periphrastic conjugation.

Allen & Greenough: §488

fpp.title: Future passive participle
fpp.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing necessity or impending action upon the subject — also called the gerundive (amandus, “about to be loved” / “to be loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and forms the passive periphrastic with esse.

Allen & Greenough: §503

future-passive-participle.title: Future passive participle
future-passive-participle.html:
A non-finite verbal adjective expressing necessity or impending action upon the subject — also called the gerundive (amandus, “about to be loved” / “to be loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and forms the passive periphrastic with esse.

Allen & Greenough: §503

act.title: Active
act.html:
The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

Allen & Greenough: §156

active.title: Active
active.html:
The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

Allen & Greenough: §156

pass.title: Passive
pass.html:
The voice in which the subject undergoes the action (liber a puero legitur, “the book is read by the boy”). Latin has distinct passive endings for the present system; the perfect passive is built from the + a form of esse.

Allen & Greenough: §156

passive.title: Passive
passive.html:
The voice in which the subject undergoes the action (liber a puero legitur, “the book is read by the boy”). Latin has distinct passive endings for the present system; the perfect passive is built from the + a form of esse.

Allen & Greenough: §156

supine.title: Supine
supine.html:
A defective verbal noun in -um (or -u), used in a narrow set of constructions: with verbs of motion to express purpose (venit spectatum, “he came to see”), and as the of a verb’s dictionary entry — the form that gives the stem for the and the future active participle.

Allen & Greenough: §508

gender.title: Gender
gender.html:
The three Latin grammatical genders are , , and . Nouns have a fixed gender; adjectives shift to match the noun they modify.

Allen & Greenough: §29

declension.title: Declension
declension.html:
One of five patterns of noun (and adjective) inflection in Latin, identified by the genitive singular ending. 1st declension: -ae (feminine, like forma). 2nd: -i (masculine and neuter). 3rd: -is (all three genders). 4th: -us (mostly masculine). 5th: -ei (mostly feminine).

Allen & Greenough: §36

principal-parts.title: Principal parts
principal-parts.html:
The set of four verb forms a dictionary entry gives so the reader can build any inflected form. For a regular verb: = the lemma’s dictionary form (1st-person singular present indicative active, e.g. amo); = present active infinitive (amare); = 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (amavi); = supine (amatum).

Allen & Greenough: §164

first-principal-part.title: First principal part
first-principal-part.html:
The first slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular present indicative active (e.g. amo, “I love”). This is the form the lemma is filed under, and it gives the present stem from which all present-system forms are built.

Allen & Greenough: §164

second-principal-part.title: Second principal part
second-principal-part.html:
The second slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the present active (e.g. amare, “to love”). It shows which the verb belongs to and how the present system inflects.

Allen & Greenough: §164

third-principal-part.title: Third principal part
third-principal-part.html:
The third slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (e.g. amavi, “I have loved”). It gives the perfect stem, from which all forms in the , , and future-perfect active are built.

Allen & Greenough: §164

fourth-principal-part.title: Fourth principal part
fourth-principal-part.html:
The fourth slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the (e.g. amatum). It gives the stem for the and the future active participle.

Allen & Greenough: §164
